.codex\I-f2-004\codex_brief_verdict.txt:3621:.codex\I-f2-004\codex_brief_audit_iter2.txt:1575:web/node_modules/next/dist/docs/01-app\02-guides\streaming.md:373:### When to use `loading.js` vs `<Suspense>`
.codex\I-f2-004\codex_brief_verdict.txt:3622:.codex\I-f2-004\codex_brief_audit_iter2.txt:1576:web/node_modules/next/dist/docs/01-app\02-guides\streaming.md:375:|                | `loading.js`                             | `<Suspense>`                     |
.codex\I-f2-004\codex_brief_verdict.txt:3623:.codex\I-f2-004\codex_brief_audit_iter2.txt:1577:web/node_modules/next/dist/docs/01-app\02-guides\streaming.md:382:Prefer explicit `<Suspense>` boundaries close to the dynamic access. When the prerenderer encounters dynamic work, it walks up the tree looking for the nearest Suspense boundary. If none is found, the build fails with a [blocking route error](/docs/messages/blocking-route). A `loading.js` high in the tree is a valid boundary, so the framework finds it and stops, but now the entire page falls back to a full-page skeleton instead of streaming granularly.
.codex\I-f2-004\codex_brief_verdict.txt:3624:.codex\I-f2-004\codex_brief_audit_iter2.txt:1578:web/node_modules/next/dist/docs/01-app\02-guides\streaming.md:393:You can start a fetch in a [Server Component](/docs/app/glossary#server-component) and pass the unresolved promise as a prop to a [Client Component](/docs/app/glossary#client-component). The promise can be passed through as many layers as needed. Only the component that calls React's [`use`](https://react.dev/reference/react/use) API to read the value needs a `<Suspense>` boundary around it:
.codex\I-f2-004\codex_brief_verdict.txt:3625:.codex\I-f2-004\codex_brief_audit_iter2.txt:1579:web/node_modules/next/dist/docs/01-app\02-guides\streaming.md:396:import { Suspense } from 'react'
.codex\I-f2-004\codex_brief_verdict.txt:3626:.codex\I-f2-004\codex_brief_audit_iter2.txt:1580:web/node_modules/next/dist/docs/01-app\02-guides\streaming.md:411:    <Suspense fallback={<p>Loading chart...</p>}>
.codex\I-f2-004\codex_brief_verdict.txt:3627:.codex\I-f2-004\codex_brief_audit_iter2.txt:1581:web/node_modules/next/dist/docs/01-app\02-guides\streaming.md:413:    </Suspense>
.codex\I-f2-004\codex_brief_verdict.txt:3628:.codex\I-f2-004\codex_brief_audit_iter2.txt:1582:web/node_modules/next/dist/docs/01-app\02-guides\streaming.md:419:import { Suspense } from 'react'
.codex\I-f2-004\codex_brief_verdict.txt:3629:.codex\I-f2-004\codex_brief_audit_iter2.txt:1583:web/node_modules/next/dist/docs/01-app\02-guides\streaming.md:432:    <Suspense fallback={<p>Loading chart...</p>}>
.codex\I-f2-004\codex_brief_verdict.txt:3630:.codex\I-f2-004\codex_brief_audit_iter2.txt:1584:web/node_modules/next/dist/docs/01-app\02-guides\streaming.md:434:    </Suspense>
.codex\I-f2-004\codex_brief_verdict.txt:3631:.codex\I-f2-004\codex_brief_audit_iter2.txt:1585:web/node_modules/next/dist/docs/01-app\02-guides\streaming.md:487:The patterns above rely on React and Suspense to stream UI. Outside of React rendering, [Route Handlers](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route) can stream raw responses using the Web Streams API. This is useful for Server-Sent Events, large file generation, or any response where you want data to arrive progressively:
.codex\I-f2-004\codex_brief_verdict.txt:3632:.codex\I-f2-004\codex_brief_audit_iter2.txt:1586:web/node_modules/next/dist/docs/01-app\02-guides\streaming.md:585:If your LCP element (a hero image, a main heading, a product photo) is inside a Suspense boundary, it can't paint until that boundary resolves. To keep LCP fast:
.codex\I-f2-004\codex_brief_verdict.txt:3633:.codex\I-f2-004\codex_brief_audit_iter2.txt:1587:web/node_modules/next/dist/docs/01-app\02-guides\streaming.md:587:- Keep LCP elements **outside** or **above** Suspense boundaries so they render as part of the static shell.
.codex\I-f2-004\codex_brief_verdict.txt:3634:.codex\I-f2-004\codex_brief_audit_iter2.txt:1588:web/node_modules/next/dist/docs/01-app\02-guides\streaming.md:589:- For non-image LCP elements (text, headings), make sure they are not wrapped in a Suspense boundary that depends on slow data.
.codex\I-f2-004\codex_brief_verdict.txt:3635:.codex\I-f2-004\codex_brief_audit_iter2.txt:1589:web/node_modules/next/dist/docs/01-app\02-guides\streaming.md:593:When a Suspense fallback is replaced by the resolved content, the browser reflows the page. If the fallback and the resolved content are different sizes, the surrounding layout shifts. To minimize CLS:
.codex\I-f2-004\codex_brief_verdict.txt:3636:.codex\I-f2-004\codex_brief_audit_iter2.txt:1590:web/node_modules/next/dist/docs/01-app\02-guides\streaming.md:596:- Use fixed or min-height containers around Suspense boundaries so the space is reserved before content arrives.
.codex\I-f2-004\codex_brief_verdict.txt:3637:.codex\I-f2-004\codex_brief_audit_iter2.txt:1591:web/node_modules/next/dist/docs/01-app\02-guides\streaming.md:600:Streaming enables [selective hydration](https://react.dev/reference/react-dom/client/hydrateRoot): React hydrates components independently as they stream in, and prioritizes hydrating whatever the user is interacting with. Each `<Suspense>` boundary is a hydration unit. Without them, React hydrates the entire page in one blocking pass. With them, hydration is broken into smaller tasks that yield to the browser, keeping the main thread responsive. The [companion demo](https://streaming-demo.labs.vercel.dev/hydration-single) lets you compare a single blocking hydration pass with [split hydration](https://streaming-demo.labs.vercel.dev/hydration-split) using Suspense boundaries.
.codex\I-f2-004\codex_brief_verdict.txt:3638:.codex\I-f2-004\codex_brief_audit_iter2.txt:1592:web/node_modules/next/dist/docs/01-app\02-guides\streaming.md:606:In the [dashboard example](#parallel-streaming-with-sibling-boundaries) above, the `<h1>` renders in the shell (good for LCP), each data section streams independently behind its own Suspense boundary (good for INP since hydration is split), and the skeleton fallbacks reserve space (good for CLS).
.codex\I-f2-004\codex_brief_verdict.txt:3639:.codex\I-f2-004\codex_brief_audit_iter2.txt:1593:web/node_modules/next/dist/docs/01-app\02-guides\streaming.md:614:When a `<Suspense>` fallback renders or a component suspends, the server must commit to `200 OK` in order to start sending the HTML stream. If a [`notFound()`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/not-found) fires mid-stream, Next.js cannot go back and change the status to 404. Instead, it injects `<meta name="robots" content="noindex">` into the streamed HTML so that search engines don't index the page. Similarly, a [`redirect()`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/redirect) mid-stream becomes a client-side redirect rather than an HTTP redirect header.
.codex\I-f2-004\codex_brief_verdict.txt:3640:.codex\I-f2-004\codex_brief_audit_iter2.txt:1594:web/node_modules/next/dist/docs/01-app\02-guides\streaming.md:618:The response body begins streaming when a Suspense fallback renders (for example, a `loading.tsx`) or when a component suspends under a `<Suspense>` boundary. To get a real HTTP status code for errors, place `notFound()` **before** any `await` or `<Suspense>` boundary:
.codex\I-f2-004\codex_brief_verdict.txt:3641:.codex\I-f2-004\codex_brief_audit_iter2.txt:1595:web/node_modules/next/dist/docs/01-app\02-guides\streaming.md:621:import { Suspense } from 'react'
.codex\I-f2-004\codex_brief_verdict.txt:3642:.codex\I-f2-004\codex_brief_audit_iter2.txt:1596:web/node_modules/next/dist/docs/01-app\02-guides\streaming.md:637:  if (!exists) notFound() // Real 404, before any Suspense boundary
.codex\I-f2-004\codex_brief_verdict.txt:3643:.codex\I-f2-004\codex_brief_audit_iter2.txt:1597:web/node_modules/next/dist/docs/01-app\02-guides\streaming.md:640:    <Suspense fallback={<p>Loading post...</p>}>
.codex\I-f2-004\codex_brief_verdict.txt:3644:.codex\I-f2-004\codex_brief_audit_iter2.txt:1598:web/node_modules/next/dist/docs/01-app\02-guides\streaming.md:642:    </Suspense>
.codex\I-f2-004\codex_brief_verdict.txt:3645:.codex\I-f2-004\codex_brief_audit_iter2.txt:1599:web/node_modules/next/dist/docs/01-app\02-guides\streaming.md:648:import { Suspense } from 'react'
.codex\I-f2-004\codex_brief_verdict.txt:3646:.codex\I-f2-004\codex_brief_audit_iter2.txt:1600:web/node_modules/next/dist/docs/01-app\02-guides\streaming.md:660:  if (!exists) notFound() // Real 404, before any Suspense boundary
.codex\I-f2-004\codex_brief_verdict.txt:3647:.codex\I-f2-004\codex_brief_audit_iter2.txt:1601:web/node_modules/next/dist/docs/01-app\02-guides\streaming.md:663:    <Suspense fallback={<p>Loading post...</p>}>
.codex\I-f2-004\codex_brief_verdict.txt:3648:.codex\I-f2-004\codex_brief_audit_iter2.txt:1602:web/node_modules/next/dist/docs/01-app\02-guides\streaming.md:665:    </Suspense>
.codex\I-f2-004\codex_brief_verdict.txt:3649:.codex\I-f2-004\codex_brief_audit_iter2.txt:1603:web/node_modules/next/dist/docs/01-app\02-guides\streaming.md:726:This section is about confirming the HTTP response is actually arriving in chunks through your infrastructure. For guidance on designing meaningful loading states and placing Suspense boundaries effectively, see [Granular streaming with `<Suspense>`](#granular-streaming-with-suspense) and the [Cache Components](/docs/app/getting-started/caching) guide.
.codex\I-f2-004\codex_brief_verdict.txt:3650:.codex\I-f2-004\codex_brief_audit_iter2.txt:1604:web/node_modules/next/dist/docs/01-app\02-guides\streaming.md:753:Run with `node stream-observer.mjs`. For a page with two sibling Suspense boundaries (like the [companion demo's Suspense page](https://streaming-demo.labs.vercel.dev/suspense-demo)), you will see output similar to:
.codex\I-f2-004\codex_brief_verdict.txt:3651:.codex\I-f2-004\codex_brief_audit_iter2.txt:1605:web/node_modules/next/dist/docs/01-app\02-guides\streaming.md:764:The `<template id="B:0">` markers are the Suspense fallback placeholders. When a boundary resolves, React streams a `<div hidden id="S:0">` containing the completed HTML and a script that swaps it into the page. The timestamps show each boundary resolving independently.
.codex\I-f2-004\codex_brief_verdict.txt:3652:.codex\I-f2-004\codex_brief_audit_iter2.txt:1606:web/node_modules/next/dist/docs/01-app\02-guides\streaming.md:781:The trigger is **your code**: async work, non-deterministic output, or runtime data. When the framework encounters these, it walks up the tree looking for a `<Suspense>` boundary to use as a fallback. Everything above those boundaries forms the [static shell](#the-static-shell), which is sent immediately. As each boundary resolves, React streams the result into the page.
.codex\I-f2-004\codex_brief_verdict.txt:3653:.codex\I-f2-004\codex_brief_audit_iter2.txt:1607:web/node_modules/next/dist/docs/01-app\02-guides\streaming.md:783:The key decisions are **what to cache** and **where to place Suspense boundaries**. Cache what you can with [`"use cache"`](/docs/app/api-reference/directives/use-cache) to grow the static shell. Push dynamic access down to the components that need it, and wrap those in `<Suspense>`. Everything else becomes part of the shell.

It distinguishes response variants (HTML vs. RSC, different prefetch types). Ensure your CDN does not strip query parameters from cache keys, as some CDNs do this by default. When the `experimental.validateRSCRequestHeaders` option is enabled and a RSC request arrives without the correct `_rsc` value, the server responds with a **307 redirect** to the URL with the correct hash. CDNs should follow this redirect. Platforms that compute the hash upstream can rewrite requests to include the correct `_rsc` before forwarding to avoid an extra round trip.

Your handler should check whether any soft tag has been invalidated after the cache entry's timestamp. The `getExpiration()` method returns the most recent revalidation timestamp across all provided tags, or `0` if none have been revalidated. Your handler should treat an entry as stale if the returned timestamp is newer than the entry's own timestamp. See the [cache handler API reference](/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/cacheHandlers#getexpiration) for the full semantics.

 succeeded in 1132ms:
   1: """HDBSCAN clustering on top-K retrieval candidate embeddings (F2 substrate).
   2: 
   3: Per Carney v6.2 §F2 Diversify-then-Verify: cluster the top-K candidate
   4: embeddings; if HDBSCAN finds more than one dense cluster the user query
   5: is ambiguous (e.g. "BPEI" → syndrome / institute / chemical clusters),
   6: and the disambiguation modal should fire. A single dense cluster (or
   7: one-cluster-allowed result) means the query is unambiguous (e.g.
   8: "tirzepatide" → all candidates about the drug).
   9: 
  10: This module is substrate-only:
  11: - Consumes pre-computed `np.ndarray` of shape (K, D); does NOT call
  12:   any embedding model at runtime (per CLAUDE.md §8.4 "no heavy ML in
  13:   autonomous loops"). Wiring into `live_retriever.py` is I-f2-003.
  14: - Wraps `hdbscan.HDBSCAN` with `allow_single_cluster=True` so a
  15:   tightly-packed candidate set returns 1 cluster rather than 0.
  16: 
  17: Pre-condition: `embeddings` should be L2-normalized for euclidean
  18: metric to be monotonic with cosine distance.
  19: """
  20: 
  21: from __future__ import annotations
  22: 
  23: from dataclasses import dataclass
  24: 
  25: import hdbscan
  26: import numpy as np
  27: 
  28: 
  29: @dataclass(frozen=True)
  30: class ClusterResult:
  31:     """Result of HDBSCAN clustering on candidate embeddings.
  32: 
  33:     Attributes:
  34:         labels: HDBSCAN cluster id per candidate (-1 = noise/outlier).
  35:         num_clusters: Count of distinct non-noise cluster ids.
  36:         is_ambiguous: True iff num_clusters > 1.
  37:     """
  38: 
  39:     labels: list[int]
  40:     num_clusters: int
  41:     is_ambiguous: bool
  42: 
  43: 
  44: def cluster_candidates(
  45:     embeddings: np.ndarray,
  46:     min_cluster_size: int = 2,
  47: ) -> ClusterResult:
  48:     """Cluster candidate embeddings with HDBSCAN.
  49: 
  50:     Args:
  51:         embeddings: Shape (K, D) float array of candidate embeddings.
  52:         min_cluster_size: HDBSCAN minimum cluster size (default 2 per
  53:             Carney v6.2 §F2 spec).
  54: 
  55:     Returns:
  56:         ClusterResult with labels, num_clusters, is_ambiguous.
  57: 
  58:     Raises:
  59:         ValueError: If `embeddings` is empty (LAW II — fail loudly).
  60: 
  61:     Edge cases:
  62:         - K < min_cluster_size: HDBSCAN cannot cluster; returns
  63:           num_clusters=0, is_ambiguous=False, labels all -1.
  64:     """
  65:     if embeddings.size == 0:
  66:         raise ValueError(
  67:             "cluster_candidates: empty embedding array; cannot cluster"
  68:         )
  69: 
  70:     n_candidates = embeddings.shape[0]
  71:     if n_candidates < min_cluster_size:
  72:         return ClusterResult(
  73:             labels=[-1] * n_candidates,
  74:             num_clusters=0,
  75:             is_ambiguous=False,
  76:         )
  77: 
  78:     clusterer = hdbscan.HDBSCAN(
  79:         min_cluster_size=min_cluster_size,
  80:         metric="euclidean",
  81:         allow_single_cluster=True,
  82:     )
  83:     raw_labels = clusterer.fit_predict(embeddings)
  84:     labels = [int(label) for label in raw_labels]
  85:     distinct_clusters = set(labels) - {-1}
  86:     num_clusters = len(distinct_clusters)
  87:     return ClusterResult(
  88:         labels=labels,
  89:         num_clusters=num_clusters,
  90:         is_ambiguous=num_clusters > 1,
  91:     )

 succeeded in 1264ms:

  13:     ClusterResult,

  15: )

  17: 
  18: def _gaussian_cloud(rng: np.random.Generator, center: tuple[float, ...], n: int, scale: float = 0.05) -> np.ndarray:
  19:     """Make `n` points centered at `center` with small Gaussian noise."""
  20:     centroid = np.array(center, dtype=np.float64)
  21:     noise = rng.normal(loc=0.0, scale=scale, size=(n, len(center)))
  22:     return centroid + noise
  23: 
  24: 
  25: def test_three_clear_clusters_ambiguous() -> None:
  26:     rng = np.random.default_rng(seed=42)
  27:     cloud_a = _gaussian_cloud(rng, (0.0, 0.0), 5)
  28:     cloud_b = _gaussian_cloud(rng, (10.0, 10.0), 5)
  29:     cloud_c = _gaussian_cloud(rng, (-10.0, -10.0), 5)
  30:     embeddings = np.vstack([cloud_a, cloud_b, cloud_c])
  31: 
  32:     result = cluster_candidates(embeddings)
  33: 
  34:     assert isinstance(result, ClusterResult)
  35:     assert result.num_clusters == 3
  36:     assert result.is_ambiguous is True
  37: 
  38: 
  39: def test_single_dense_cluster_unambiguous() -> None:
  40:     rng = np.random.default_rng(seed=7)
  41:     embeddings = _gaussian_cloud(rng, (0.0, 0.0), 10)
  42: 
  43:     result = cluster_candidates(embeddings)
  44: 
  45:     assert result.num_clusters == 1
  46:     assert result.is_ambiguous is False
  47: 
  48: 
  49: def test_two_clusters_ambiguous() -> None:

  51:     cloud_a = _gaussian_cloud(rng, (0.0, 0.0), 5)
  52:     cloud_b = _gaussian_cloud(rng, (10.0, 10.0), 5)
  53:     embeddings = np.vstack([cloud_a, cloud_b])
  54:

  56: 
  57:     assert result.num_clusters == 2
  58:     assert result.is_ambiguous is True
